result:
The tide has retreated just far enough to leave its secrets exposed, and the first gray light finds the pools already awake. Steam curls faintly off the wet rocks where the night's cold meets morning's first warmth, dissolving into a sky the color of an oyster shell. Each pool sits like a scattered mirror in the black basalt, holding its own small ocean, its own weather, separate from the great one grumbling somewhere beyond the fog.

In the shallowest basin, a hermit crab drags its borrowed shell across a bed of pink coralline algae, leaving a faint scrape in the film of diatoms. Anemones have opened overnight, their tentacles unfurled into soft green stars, waiting with the patience of something that has never known hurry. A sculpin, mottled the exact brown of the rock it hides against, holds so still that only the pulse of its gills gives it away.

Barnacles crowd the pool's rim in white thickets, closed tight, indifferent to the retreating tide, though a few have begun to crack their plates open, feathery legs combing the thin film of water for anything the night left behind. Below them, a chiton clings flat and armored, unmoving as stone. Purple sea urchins huddle in a shadowed crevice, spines trembling faintly with some current only they can feel.

Light comes slowly, sliding along the wet rock in a thin gold line before it reaches the water itself, and when it does, the whole pool ignites — every grain of sand, every strand of eelgrass, every fleck of mica catching fire for a moment before dawn fully arrives. Gulls cry somewhere up the beach, impatient for the tide's return. But here, in this small suspended world, everything moves at the pace of water finding its own stillness, indifferent to the hour, waiting only for the sea to come back and claim it.
